narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw a 41% chance of you not passing the bar exam, leading to this tough call on your application. Picture your profile coming together like chapters in a story: the strongest signal was your Year 3 decile of 2.0, which stood out as the biggest pull toward struggle, suggesting those later grades painted a picture of mounting challenges in a demanding program. Right behind that was your LSAT score of 29.5, which the model flagged as another heavy influence tipping the scales toward failure, like a foundational hurdle that sets the tone for the whole journey. Your Year 1 decile of 3.0 added to that narrative, reinforcing a pattern of being in the lower third early on. Even your middle family income bracket played a role, as it nudged the outlook downward compared to others with more financial cushions for prep courses or extra support. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.0 wove in similarly, showing steady but not standout academic momentum coming into law school. Factors like your white racial background, being female, and planning full-time studies all contributed their own subtle pushes toward that riskier picture the model assembled. It's not that any one thing defined you—it's how they combined to tell a story of higher odds against beating the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential or heart; it's just the model's honest read based on patterns from thousands like you. If you boost those deciles or LSAT in retakes, or build more academic momentum, the story could shift dramatically next time. You've got this drive—keep pushing, and law school might still be in your future. With empathy, Your Admissions Advisor
